summary: A consequentialist ethical theory that advocates maximizing happiness for the greatest number of people. Founded by Jeremy Bentham and developed by John Stuart Mill. Comes in 'act' and 'rule' forms, and has been critiqued by Bernard Williams, Derek Parfit, and others.

works.name: The Greatest Happiness Principle & Core Doctrine
works.description: The foundational principle of utilitarianism, which holds that actions are right insofar as they promote the greatest happiness for the greatest number. Developed by Jeremy Bentham in An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation and refined by John Stuart Mill in his essay Utilitarianism. Bentham's felicific calculus attempted to quantify pleasure and pain, while Mill distinguished between higher and lower pleasures. The doctrine has been traced to Cesare Beccaria's On Crimes and Punishments.

clues.clue: advocates 'the greatest happiness for the greatest number'
examples: what ethical position developed by Jeremy Bentham advocates the greatest good for the greatest number?
actions should maximize human happiness

examples: the founder of this school, who theorized a circular prison called the panopticon, was Jeremy Bentham

clues.clue: John Stuart Mill wrote a treatise titled Utilitarianism; distinguished higher and lower pleasures
examples: A John Stuart Mill treatise is titled after this school of philosophy

clues.clue: comes in 'act' and 'rule' forms

clues.clue: Cesare Beccaria likely originated the phrase 'greatest happiness of the greatest number' in On Crimes and Punishments
examples: Bentham likely took the phrase 'the greatest happiness of the greatest number' from the work of this enlightenment criminologist

works.name: Act vs. Rule Utilitarianism
works.description: The distinction between act utilitarianism (judging each individual action by its consequences) and rule utilitarianism (judging actions by whether they conform to rules that maximize utility). J.J.C. Smart advocated 'extreme' act utilitarianism, while R.M. Hare argued rule utilitarianism was preferable for ordinary people. John Rawls's 'Two Concepts of Rules' defended utilitarianism by distinguishing justifying a practice from justifying an act within it. Smart criticized rule utilitarianism as 'restricted.'

clues.clue: act vs. rule distinction — act judges individual actions, rule judges underlying principles
examples: Smart contrasted 'extreme' act utilitarianism with this other approach, which judges actions based on their underlying principles

clues.clue: J.J.C. Smart advocated extreme/act utilitarianism; criticized rule utilitarianism as 'restricted'
examples: J.J.C. Smart criticized this utilitarian school as 'restricted'

clues.clue: John Rawls's 'Two Concepts of Rules' defended utilitarianism from punishment and promise-keeping objections
examples: this thinker defended utilitarianism from 'punishment' and 'promise-keeping' objections in 'Two Concepts of Rules'

clues.clue: R.M. Hare argued rule utilitarianism preferable for 'proles' needing intuitive-level moral principles
examples: this theory is preferable for 'proles' who need 'intuitive level' moral principles because they lack the cognitive abilities of 'archangels'

clues.clue: 'preference' utilitarianism pioneered by Peter Singer
examples: generally, 'effective altruism' advocates adopt this position, which comes in... a 'preference' form pioneered by Peter Singer

works.name: Bernard Williams's Critiques
works.description: Bernard Williams was the most prominent critic of utilitarianism in the quizbowl corpus. He debated J.J.C. Smart in the volume Utilitarianism: For and Against, attacking the doctrine of 'negative responsibility' through thought experiments like 'Jim and the Indians' and 'George and the chemical warfare researcher.' Williams coined the term 'moral luck' in a paper about Gauguin's abandonment of his family, and introduced the phrase 'one thought too many' to critique utilitarian prescriptions.

clues.clue: 'one thought too many' — critiques utilitarian prescriptions for a man choosing between saving his wife or a stranger
examples: Bernard Williams coined this four-word phrase for the prescriptions that moral theories offer a man who can either save his wife or a stranger

works.name: Derek Parfit & the Repugnant Conclusion
works.description: Derek Parfit's 'repugnant conclusion' (from the mere addition paradox) is a major counterexample to utilitarianism. It shows that for any population, there exists a larger population whose total existence is better even though individuals have lives barely worth living. Parfit explored this in Reasons and Persons, which also uses the teletransporter thought experiment. Parfit later attempted to show that rule utilitarianism, Kantian deontology, and Scanlon's contractualism make similar recommendations in On What Matters.

clues.clue: repugnant conclusion / mere addition paradox — larger population with lives barely worth living could have more total utility
examples: a challenge to this position that results from the 'mere addition paradox' was elucidated in the book Reasons and Persons by Derek Parfit
for a population of at least 10 billion, there could exist some larger population whose sum-total existence would be better

examples: this thinker used the 'teletransporter' thought experiment in his book Reasons and Persons

clues.clue: On What Matters — attempted to show rule utilitarianism, Kantian deontology, and Scanlon's contractualism converge
examples: this ethicist attempted to show that rule utilitarianism, Kantian deontology, and T. M. Scanlon's contractualism all make similar recommendations

works.name: Peter Singer & Effective Altruism
works.description: Peter Singer is a major modern utilitarian, known for 'Famine, Affluence, and Morality' (1971/1972), which uses the drowning child analogy to argue that wealthy people are morally obligated to donate to humanitarian causes. Singer coined the term 'speciesism' and argued that the capability to suffer should be the basis for rights. He is closely associated with the effective altruism movement and has shifted from preference utilitarianism to hedonistic utilitarianism.

clues.clue: Peter Singer — drowning child analogy, 'Famine, Affluence, and Morality,' effective altruism
examples: this philosopher used an analogy involving saving a drowning child to argue that charitable giving is obligatory
generally, 'effective altruism' advocates adopt this position... a 'preference' form pioneered by Peter Singer

examples: this thinker argued in a 1975 book that the capability to suffer should be the basis for rights, instead of the 'speciesist' grounding in intelligence

clues.clue: effective altruism movement — using reason to benefit as many people as possible

works.name: Henry Sidgwick & The Methods of Ethics
works.description: Henry Sidgwick's The Methods of Ethics (1874) analyzes three approaches to ethics: egoism, intuitionism, and utilitarianism. Sidgwick is commended by G.E. Moore for not committing the naturalistic fallacy. Sidgwick identified the paradox of hedonism and formulated the principle of 'pure equality' (each counts for one). He is frequently clued as a key figure bridging classical utilitarianism and modern moral philosophy.

clues.clue: The Methods of Ethics — three approaches: egoism, intuitionism, utilitarianism
examples: these are the two thinkers most discussed as exemplars of a view contrasted with intuitionism and egoism in Henry Sidgwick's The Methods of Ethics

clues.clue: G.E. Moore praised Sidgwick for not committing the naturalistic fallacy

clues.clue: paradox of hedonism; principle of 'pure equality' (each counts for one)
examples: Parfit's principle of 'each counts for one' may have been inspired by this thinker's principle of 'pure equality'

works.name: Critiques and Thought Experiments
works.description: Utilitarianism faces numerous thought-experiment-based critiques. Robert Nozick's 'utility monster' shows that a being who derives vastly more pleasure could justify taking from others. The trolley problem (Philippa Foot) was originally an argument against utilitarianism. The 'demandingness objection' holds that utilitarianism demands too much of individuals. 'Roko's Basilisk' imagines an AI punishing those who did not help build it. The 'no rest' objection (Louis Pojman) argues strict utilitarianism freezes adherents into inaction.

clues.clue: Robert Nozick's 'utility monster' — a being that gains more utility, so maximizing total utility means giving it everything
examples: Robert Nozick imagined a scenario in which it is more readily acquired by a 'monster'

clues.clue: trolley problem (Philippa Foot) — originally an argument against utilitarianism
examples: Foot's trolley problem originally served as an argument against this consequentialist ethical system

clues.clue: 'no rest' objection — strict adherence causes inaction
examples: anyone trying to adopt it strictly would be frozen into inaction because it allows 'no rest'

clues.clue: 'demandingness objection' — utilitarianism demands too much
examples: the inability of this view to accommodate supererogation is known as the 'demandingness objection'

clues.clue: Roko's Basilisk — AI punishing those who did not help build it
examples: 'Roko's Basilisk' refers to the fear that an AI adopting this position would punish those who did not help build it

examples: this philosopher, who called Bentham a leather-tongued 'arch-philistine'

works.name: General / Identification
works.description: Standard identification clues for utilitarianism as a concept: it is a consequentialist ethical theory, contrasted with deontology. Bentham and Mill are its co-founders. The felicific calculus is Bentham's tool for measuring pleasure. It is associated with the phrase 'greatest happiness principle.'

comprehensive_summary:
Utilitarianism is the most heavily clued ethical theory in quizbowl. It is a consequentialist system that holds the morality of an action depends on its outcomes, specifically whether it maximizes happiness for the greatest number. The theory was founded by Jeremy Bentham, who developed the felicific calculus to quantify pleasure and pain in An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation, and who described humanity as governed by 'two sovereign masters, pain and pleasure.' John Stuart Mill refined the doctrine in his 1861 essay Utilitarianism, distinguishing between higher and lower pleasures and famously claiming 'better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied.' The phrase 'greatest happiness of the greatest number' may trace back to Cesare Beccaria's On Crimes and Punishments.

The theory comes in 'act' and 'rule' forms. Act utilitarianism judges each individual action by its consequences, while rule utilitarianism judges actions by whether they conform to rules that maximize utility. J.J.C. Smart advocated extreme act utilitarianism and criticized rule utilitarianism as 'restricted.' John Rawls defended utilitarianism from objections in 'Two Concepts of Rules.' Peter Singer pioneered 'preference' utilitarianism and is closely associated with the effective altruism movement.

Bernard Williams is the most prominent critic in the quizbowl corpus. He debated Smart in Utilitarianism: For and Against, introducing the 'Jim and the Indians' and 'George and the chemical warfare researcher' thought experiments to challenge the doctrine of 'negative responsibility.' Williams coined the term 'moral luck' and the phrase 'one thought too many.' Derek Parfit's 'repugnant conclusion' (from the mere addition paradox in Reasons and Persons) is another major counterexample, showing that utilitarianism could endorse a vastly overpopulated world of miserable people. Robert Nozick's 'utility monster' and Philippa Foot's trolley problem also serve as critiques. The 'no rest' objection holds that strict utilitarianism freezes adherents into inaction, and the 'demandingness objection' argues it asks too much.

Henry Sidgwick's The Methods of Ethics is a key text that analyzes egoism, intuitionism, and utilitarianism as three methods of ethics. G.E. Moore praised Sidgwick for not committing the naturalistic fallacy. In modern times, Peter Singer's drowning child analogy and effective altruism have made utilitarianism prominent in applied ethics.

cards.front: Concept: the 'mere addition paradox' shows that for any population, a larger one whose members have lives barely worth living could have more total utility

Philosopher: debated J.J.C. Smart in a volume subtitled 'For and Against' and advanced the 'integrity objection' using the thought experiments of Jim and the Indians and George the chemical warfare researcher
Philosopher: coined the term 'moral luck' in an essay arguing that we judge Gauguin's abandonment of his family based on how successful his art happened to be
Philosopher: coined the phrase 'one thought too many' for the utilitarian prescription that a man should calculate before saving his wife over a stranger
Concept: an imagined being that derives vastly more pleasure than others, so maximizing total utility means giving it everything
Concept: the objection that strict adherence to maximizing utility would freeze a person into inaction because it allows 'no rest'

Philosopher: defended utilitarianism from 'punishment' and 'promise-keeping' objections in 'Two Concepts of Rules' and later introduced the 'original position' in A Theory of Justice
Philosopher: used the drowning child analogy to argue wealthy people are morally obligated to donate to humanitarian causes in 'Famine, Affluence, and Morality'
Philosopher: coined the term 'speciesism' and argued the capability to suffer, not intelligence, should be the basis for moral rights
Work: analyzes three methods of ethics — egoism, intuitionism, and utilitarianism — and its author is praised by G.E. Moore for not committing the naturalistic fallacy
